content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
Mr. Douglas Eads 
Fremont City Clerk 
Administration 
322·5660 
City Government Building 
Fremont, CA 
94538 
Re: 
Our No. A-82-ll2 
Dear Mr. Eads: 
Executive/legal 
322.5901 
July 22, 1982 
Enforcement 
322-6«1 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your letter requesting information on 
behalf of Fremont City Planning Commissioner John Dutra. 
In your letter, you describe various types of financial 
interests held by Mr. Dutra and ask how he should report 
them on his Statement of Economic Interests. 
Mr. Dutra has 
already filed his 1981 Statement, but you indicate that he 
is willing to amend his Statement ~f necessary. 
FACTS 
Mr. Dutra and his wife jointly own approximately 62% of 
Dutra Realty Enterprises, Inc. 
You did not specify the 
exact ownership interest held by each. 
Dutra Realty is the 
sole owner of three businesses: 
Guardian Home Loans, Red 
Carpet Real Estate of Mission and Red Carpet Real Estate of 
Warm Springs. 
These businesses, in turn, own real property 
which generates rental income. 
DISCUSSION 
A. 
Dutra Realty. 
As you indicate in your letter, Mr. Dutra is required 
to disclose, as an investment, the ownership interest he 
and his wife have in Dutra Realty. 
(Government Code 
Sections 82034 and 87206.1/) 
This is reported on Schedule 
A ("Investments"). 
-
1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


Mr. Douglas Eads 
July 22, 1982 
Page 2 
B. 
Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet of Mission and Red 
Carpet of Warm Springs. 
You state in your letter that Guardian Home Loans, Red 
Carpet of Mission and Red Carpet of Warm Springs are 
wholly-owned by Dutra Realty, a company in which the Dutras 
have a 62% interest. 
Section 82034 states that an 
investment of an individual includes a pro rata share of 
investments of any business entity in which the individual 
or immediate family owns a 10% or greater interest. 
Therefore, the Dutras must report, as an investment, their 
pro rata share of each of the three companies owned by 
Dutra Realty, if the fair market value of the share is 
worth $1,000 or more. 
This is reported on Schedule C 
("Investments and Interests in Real Property Held By 
Business Entities or Trusts"). 
C. 
Real Property Held by Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet 
of Mission and Red Carpet of Warm Springs. 
You correctly state in your letter that Mr. Dutra must 
report real property held by the three business entities if 
his pro rata share (62%) of the value of the property 
exceeds $1,000. 
Section 82033 provides: 
, . 
Interests in real property of an individual 
includes a pro rata share of interest in real property 
of any business entity ... in which the individual or 
immediate family owns ... a 10 percent interest or 
greater. 
The property should be reported on Schedule C 
("Investments and Interests in Real Property Held By 
Business Entities or Trusts"). 
Mr. Dutra may want to 
attach an additional sheet to Schedule C so that he can 
clearly indicate that the real property is held by 
companies that are owned by Dutra Realty, rather than by 
Dutra Realty itself. 
D. 
Rental Income From the Real Property Held by Guardian 
Home Loans, Red Carpet of Mission and Red Carpet of 
Warm Springs. 
You provided me with the method that Mr. Dutra has been 
using to determine when rental income must be reported. 
The method he is using is incorrect. 


Mr. Douglas Eads 
July 22, 1982 
Page 3 
Mr. Dutra should consider the rental income generated 
by the real property that is owned by Guardian Home Loans, 
Red Carpet of Mission and Red Carpet of Warm Springs to be 
part of the gross income of the business owning the 
property. 
Mr. Dutra must then report, on Schedule D 
("Income"), his pro rata share of the gross receipts of 
each of the three business entities. 
For purposes of reporting income, the percentage 
comprising Mr. Dutra's pro rata share is determined by 
adding the percentage of the business that Mr. Dutra owns 
with one-half of his wife's interest.~/ 
In addition, Mr. Dutra must report, on Schedule H 
("Income to Business Entities"), the name of any tenant who 
pays so much in rent to any of the three busine~s entities 
that Mr. Dutra1s pro rata share1/ of such rent is $10,000 
or more. 
E. 
Equity Advances. 
You indicate that the real estate firms make loans to 
individuals that are secured by real property. 
These loans 
are called "equity advances." 
Mr.' Dutra asks how these 
should be reported. 
The equity advances are interests in real property4/ 
and must be reported on Schedule C ("Investments and 
-
2/ 
In disclosing income and sources of income, only 
the filer's interest plus the filer's community property 
share in his or her spouse's interest (1/2) is included in 
calculating the filer's pro rata share. 
For example, if Mr. Dutra and his wife equally share 
their 62% interest in the business, then Mr. Dutra's pro 
rata share of the business' income is 46.5% (31% plus 
15.5%) • 
In reporting interests other than income, the filer's 
pro rata share includes the filer's share plus the full 
interest of immediate family members. 
(In this case, 62%.) 
1/ See footnote 2. 
i/ They are treated as real property, rather than as 
an investment, because the loan is secured with real 
property_ 


Mr. Douglas Eads 
July 22, 1982 
Page 4 
Interests in Real Property Held By Business Entities or 
Trusts") if Mr. Dutra's pro rata share (62%) of the value 
of the loan is worth $1,000 or more. 
In addition, interest and repayment of the principal 
received by the business entity that extended the loan are 
added to the gross income of the business entity. 
Mr. Dutra must then report his pro rata share5/ in the 
gross income of the business entity on Schedule D 
("Income") . 
Lastly, Mr. Dutra must report on Schedule H the name of 
any person who provided so much in interest and repayment 
on loans to the business entity that Mr. Dutra's pro rata 
share~/ of the payments amounted to $10,000 or more. 
F. 
Second Notes and Deeds of Trust. 
You indicate in your letter that Guardian Home Loans 
holds second notes and deeds of trust and you ask the same 
reporting questions that you did with regard to equity 
advances. 
A second note and deed of trus,t. is reporte¢l as an 
interest in real property on Schedule C if Mr. Dutra's pro 
rata share (62%) of the value of the note and deed of trust 
is $1,000 or more. 
Interest and repayment on a note and deed of trust are 
included in the gross income of Guardian Home Loans. 
Mr. Dutra must then report his pro rata share7/ of the 
gross income on Schedule D. 
-
The names of any individuals providing so much in 
interest and repayment of principal to the business that 
Mr. Dutra's pro rata share8/ of the amount paid is $10,000 
or more should be reported-on Schedule H. 
1/ See footnote 2. 
~/ See footnote 2. 
1/ See footnote 2. 
~/ See footnote 2. 


Mr. Douglas Eads 
July 22, 1982 
Page 5 
G. 
Unsecured loans to salespeople. 
Dutra Realty makes unsecured loans to its agents to aid 
them financially prior to the closing of escrow. 
A loan to 
an individual is not an investment and is not reportable on 
Schedule C.2/ 
Payment of the interest on the loans and the repayment 
of principal makes the salespersons sources of income to 
Dutra Realty. 
Sources of income to a business are reported 
at the $10,000 threshold amount and, thus, Mr. Dutra must 
report the names of any salesperson who provided so much in 
payments during the reporting period that Mr. Dutra's pro 
rata sharelOI of the payments amounted to $10,000 or more. 
H. 
Commissions and Salary Income. 
Mr. Dutra received sales commissions and salary from 
Dutra Realty. 
These are reported on Schedule D ("Income") 
as income from Dutra Realty. 
In addition, Mr. Dutra must 
report his pro rata sharelll of the gross income of Dutra 
Realty. 
This is also reported on Schedule D. 
You are correct in stating that the sources of the 
commissions (the sellers) are reportable only where 
Mr. Dutra's pro rata share of the gross commissions paid 
Dutra Realty by individual sellers amounts to $10,000 or 
more.121 
21 
Whereas a loan to a business entity is reportable 
as an investment. 
A loan secured by real property is reportable as an 
interest in real property whether made to an individual or 
a business entity. 
101 
See footnote 2. 
III See footnote 2. 
121 
Mr. Dutra's pro rata share is not the 62% you 
indicate, but rather it is calculated by the method 
explained in footnote 2. 


Mr. Douglas Eads 
July 22, 1982 
Page 6 
I hope I have clarified the reporting requirements of 
the Political Reform Act for Mr. Dutra. 
I am including a 
copy of his original Statement of Economic Interests and a 
new copy so that he can make the appropriate amendments to 
his Statement. 
I appreciate the efforts Mr. Dutra is taking to 
properly complete his Statement. 
If I can be of any 
additional help to him, please feel free to contact me at 
916/322-5901. 
JSM: km 
Very truly yours, 
~~./II9f~1i/ 
~Janis Shank McLean 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


June 8, 1982 
Ms. Alice Hughes, Documents Examiner 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Dear Ms. Hughes: 
9 9 
On February 3, 1982, you participated in a conference telephone call relating 
to disclosure of economic interest with Planning Commissioner John Dutra, as 
well as myself and other staff members. 
We appreciate your help in clarifying 
some requirements for us, and we fully understand your reluctance to tackle 
some of the questions in a telephone conversation. 
Since that time Mr. Dutra 
has filed an annual statement which complies with his understanding of the 
law. 
However, considering the extent and complexity of Mr. Dutra's interests, 
he wishes to both provide explanatory information to the Commission, and to 
seek clarification of his disclosure obligations. 
Mr. Dutra and his wife Bernadine jointly own approximately 62% of Dutra Realty 
Enterprises, Inc., which we understand is subject to disclosure as an 
investment. 
Dutra Realty, in turn, is the sole owner of three businesses: 
Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet Real Estate of Mission, and Red Carpet Real 
Estate of Warm Springs. It is understood that Mr. Dutra's interest in these 
businesses must be disclosed. 
It is also understood that Mr. Dutra must 
disclose real property owned by these businesses where his pro rata share 
(62%) of the value exceeds $1,000. 
It is our understanding that rental income 
from these properties must be disclosed on Schedule H when Mr. Dutra's 
pro rata share (62%) of the income exceeds $10,000 ($10,000 ~ .62 = $16,129). 
Eguity Advances: 
The real estate firms occasionally loan money to individuals 
in the form of "equity advances," secured by real property. 
Does each of 
these loans constitute a reportable investment of the realty company? 
If so, 
is the reporting threshold reached when Mr. Dutra's pro rata share (62%) of 
the investment (loan) is worth $1,000 or more ($1,000 ~ .62 = $1,613)? Are 
these loans more properly reported as investments or rather as interest in 
real property held by business entities? All equity advances were reported on 
Schedule C. 
Would interest income to the realty company from these equity advances be 
treated as business income ( 
H)? If so, would the disclosure 
threshold be reached when Mr. Dutra's pro rata share (62%) reach $10,000 
($10,000 ~ .62 = $16,129)? 
Mr. Dutra included principal and interest on these 
equity advances on Schedule C. 


Ms. Alice Hughes - FPPC 
John Dutra Disclosure Statements 
June 8, 1982 
Page 2 
Second Notes and Deeds of Trust: 
Guardian Home Loans owns second notes and 
deeds of trust. 
The same questions posed above with regard to the equity 
advances would apply here. 
These were reported on Schedule C. 
Associate Loans: 
Dutra Realty Enterprises, Inc. makes unsecured loans to 
their salesmen only. 
These loans are used to tide agents over when escrows 
have not closed. 
Are these reportable on Schedule "c" as investments? 
Is the 
reportable threshold reached when Mr. Dutra's pro rata share (62%) of these 
investments reaches $1,000 ($1,000 ~ .62 = $1,613)? 
I would also pose the 
same question with regard to interest income as raised in connection with the 
"equity advances." 
These loans have not been reported. 
Commission and Salary Income: 
Mr. Dutra received sales commissions and salary 
from Dutra Realty Enterprises, Inc., which we understand must be disclosed. 
Although Mr. Dutra receives commissions based upon the sales he is personally 
responsible for, the total commission paid by the seller to Dutra Realty 
Enterprises, Inc. is reduced by certain expenses of the business before being 
paid to Mr. Dutra. 
Therefore, our understanding is that this commission 
income is reportable by Mr. Dutra as personal income from Dutra Realty 
Enterprises. However, the source of the commissions (the seller) need only be 
disclosed when Mr. Dutra's pro rata share (62%) of the gross commission paid 
to Dutra Realty Enterprises, Inc. exceeds $10,000 ($10,000 ~ .62 = $16,129). 
Mr. Dutra has reported all commissions paid to the firm which totaled $10,000 
or more from an individual buyer or seller. 
Mr. Dutra's recent Statement of Economic Interest was filed based upon his 
understanding of the requirements pursuant to our phone conversation and other 
information provided to him. 
He has indicated his intention to amend that 
statement should your response to this letter indicate to him that he has not 
fully disclosed. 
DE:pm/MSa-64 
cc: 
John Dutra 


Commissioll 
95814 
r.

sections.question: In your letter, you describe various types of financial interests held by Mr. Dutra and ask how he should report them on his Statement of Economic Interests. Specific questions address: (1) reporting of Dutra Realty Enterprises ownership; (2) reporting of pro rata shares in Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet Real Estate of Mission, and Red Carpet Real Estate of Warm Springs; (3) reporting of real property held by these business entities; (4) reporting of rental income from real property; (5) reporting of equity advances secured by real property; (6) reporting of second notes and deeds of trust; (7) reporting of unsecured loans to salespeople; and (8) reporting of commissions and salary income.
sections.conclusion: Mr. Dutra must report: (A) his and his wife's 62% ownership interest in Dutra Realty on Schedule A; (B) pro rata shares of the three companies wholly-owned by Dutra Realty on Schedule C if worth $1,000 or more; (C) real property held by these entities on Schedule C if pro rata share exceeds $1,000; (D) pro rata share of gross income from the three business entities on Schedule D and tenant names on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold; (E) equity advances as real property interests on Schedule C if $1,000 or more and borrower names on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold; (F) second notes and deeds of trust as real property interests on Schedule C and obligor names on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold; (G) unsecured salesperson loans not reportable on Schedule C but obligor names reportable on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold; and (H) commissions, salary, and pro rata share of gross income on Schedule D with seller names reportable on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold.

sections.question_synthetic: A Fremont City Planning Commissioner seeks clarification on how to report various complex financial interests held through multiple business entities on his Statement of Economic Interests, including ownership interests, real property, rental income, secured loans, and other financial arrangements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provides detailed guidance that Mr. Dutra must report his complex business interests across multiple schedules (A, C, D, and H) with varying thresholds ($1,000 for investments and real property interests, $10,000 for income sources), treating different asset types differently based on whether they are secured, income-generating, or direct holdings. The reporting of pro rata shares is calculated differently for income versus non-income interests, with only the filer's interest plus half the spouse's community property share counted for income purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (June 8, 1982) and the FPPC response letter (July 22, 1982). Response includes extensive footnotes explaining pro rata share calculations and distinctions between income and non-income reporting thresholds. Original request letter provides context for the technical legal questions posed.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In your letter, you describe various types of financial interests held by Mr. Dutra and ask how he should report them on his Statement of Economic Interests. Specific questions address: (1) reporting of Dutra Realty Enterprises ownership; (2) reporting of pro rata shares in Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet Real Estate of Mission, and Red Carpet Real Estate of Warm Springs; (3) reporting of real property held by these business entities; (4) reporting of rental income from real property; (5) reporting of equity advances secured by real property; (6) reporting of second notes and deeds of trust; (7) reporting of unsecured loans to salespeople; and (8) reporting of commissions and salary income.

CONCLUSION: Mr. Dutra must report: (A) his and his wife's 62% ownership interest in Dutra Realty on Schedule A; (B) pro rata shares of the three companies wholly-owned by Dutra Realty on Schedule C if worth $1,000 or more; (C) real property held by these entities on Schedule C if pro rata share exceeds $1,000; (D) pro rata share of gross income from the three business entities on Schedule D and tenant names on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold; (E) equity advances as real property interests on Schedule C if $1,000 or more and borrower names on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold; (F) second notes and deeds of trust as real property interests on Schedule C and obligor names on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold; (G) unsecured salesperson loans not reportable on Schedule C but obligor names reportable on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold; and (H) commissions, salary, and pro rata share of gross income on Schedule D with seller names reportable on Schedule H at $10,000 threshold.

In your letter, you describe various types of financial interests held by Mr. Dutra and ask how he should report them on his Statement of Economic Interests. Mr. Dutra has already filed his 1981 Statement, but you indicate that he is willing to amend his Statement ~f necessary. FACTS Mr. Dutra and his wife jointly own approximately 62% of Dutra Realty Enterprises, Inc. You did not specify the exact ownership interest held by each. Dutra Realty is the sole owner of three businesses: Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet Real Estate of Mission and Red Carpet Real Estate of Warm Springs. These businesses, in turn, own real property which generates rental income. DISCUSSION A. Dutra Realty. As you indicate in your letter, Mr. Dutra is required to disclose, as an investment, the ownership interest he and his wife have in Dutra Realty. (Government Code Sections 82034 and 87206.1/) This is reported on Schedule A ("Investments"). - 1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Mr. Douglas Eads July 22, 1982 Page 2 B. Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet of Mission and Red Carpet of Warm Springs. You state in your letter that Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet of Mission and Red Carpet of Warm Springs are wholly-owned by Dutra Realty, a company in which the Dutras have a 62% interest. Section 82034 states that an investment of an individual includes a pro rata share of investments of any business entity in which the individual or immediate family owns a 10% or greater interest. Therefore, the Dutras must report, as an investment, their pro rata share of each of the three companies owned by Dutra Realty, if the fair market value of the share is worth $1,000 or more. This is reported on Schedule C ("Investments and Interests in Real Property Held By Business Entities or Trusts"). C. Real Property Held by Guardian Home Loans, Red Carpet of Mission and Red Carpet of Warm Springs. You correctly state in your letter that Mr. Dutra must report real property held by the three business entities if his pro rata share (62%) of the value of the property exceeds $1,000. Section 82033 provides: , . Interests in real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interest in real property of any business entity ... in which the individual or immediate family owns ... a 10 percent interest or greater. The property should be reported on Schedule C ("Investments and Interests in Real Property Held By Business Entities or Trusts"). Mr. Dutra may want to attach an additional sheet to Schedule C so
embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Janis Shank McLean responds to Fremont City Clerk Douglas Eads regarding Planning Commissioner John Dutra's Statement of Economic Interests disclosure obligations for complex ownership interests in Dutra Realty Enterprises and its subsidiary businesses. The letter provides detailed guidance on reporting requirements for investments, real property, business income, and various loan arrangements across multiple disclosure schedules.
